raw_response:
{
  "communicative_function": "The statement functions as a self-reflective declaration of a practice aimed at cultivating metacognitive awareness and mental discipline, distinguishing between passive mental events and active acceptance or alignment.",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "There is a meaningful distinction between thoughts that arise spontaneously (occurrence) and thoughts that one chooses to accept or identify with (endorsement).",
    "The speaker has agency in choosing which thoughts to endorse.",
    "This distinction can be learned and practiced.",
    "Non-endorsed thoughts can exist without being acted upon or believed.",
    "Consciousness is separable from the content of thought."
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "The statement is internally coherent. It logically distinguishes between two cognitive states — passive occurrence and active endorsement — and asserts a practice of recognizing that distinction, which is consistent with common models of mindfulness or cognitive behavioral frameworks.",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": [
    "The speaker has the capacity for metacognitive awareness (i.e., ability to observe their own thoughts).",
    "The speaker experiences thoughts that they do not wish to automatically endorse.",
    "The practice leads to greater mental clarity, reduced reactivity, or improved alignment with chosen values.",
    "The distinction between occurrence and endorsement is experientially accessible."
  ],
  "conditions_for_misleading": [
    "If thoughts and endorsements are fundamentally inseparable or if all thoughts carry implicit endorsement, the distinction is illusory.",
    "If the practice reinforces a false sense of a separate 'self' that is entirely independent of thought, it could obscure insights about non-dual awareness.",
    "If used to justify dissociation from responsibility (e.g., 'I don't endorse my harmful thought, so it doesn't matter'), it may be misleading."
  ]
}

parsed_json.communicative_function: The statement functions as a self-reflective declaration of a practice aimed at cultivating metacognitive awareness and mental discipline, distinguishing between passive mental events and active acceptance or alignment.
implicit_claims: There is a meaningful distinction between thoughts that arise spontaneously (occurrence) and thoughts that one chooses to accept or identify with (endorsement).
The speaker has agency in choosing which thoughts to endorse.
This distinction can be learned and practiced.
Non-endorsed thoughts can exist without being acted upon or believed.
Consciousness is separable from the content of thought.
parsed_json.coherence_assessment: The statement is internally coherent. It logically distinguishes between two cognitive states — passive occurrence and active endorsement — and asserts a practice of recognizing that distinction, which is consistent with common models of mindfulness or cognitive behavioral frameworks.
conditions_for_usefulness: The speaker has the capacity for metacognitive awareness (i.e., ability to observe their own thoughts).
The speaker experiences thoughts that they do not wish to automatically endorse.
The practice leads to greater mental clarity, reduced reactivity, or improved alignment with chosen values.
The distinction between occurrence and endorsement is experientially accessible.
conditions_for_misleading: If thoughts and endorsements are fundamentally inseparable or if all thoughts carry implicit endorsement, the distinction is illusory.
If the practice reinforces a false sense of a separate 'self' that is entirely independent of thought, it could obscure insights about non-dual awareness.
If used to justify dissociation from responsibility (e.g., 'I don't endorse my harmful thought, so it doesn't matter'), it may be misleading.
